questions.question: Which flight instruments operate from the pitot-static system?
questions.correct_answer: Airspeed indicator, altimeter, and vertical speed indicator
distractors: Airspeed indicator, heading indicator, and altimeter
Attitude indicator, altimeter, and turn coordinator
Airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, and VSI
questions.explanation: The pitot-static system powers three instruments: the airspeed indicator (pitot + static), altimeter (static only), and vertical speed indicator (static only).

questions.question: What does the altimeter setting (Kollsman window) compensate for?
questions.explanation: The Kollsman window allows the pilot to set the current altimeter setting (barometric pressure), compensating for nonstandard pressure so the altimeter reads correct altitude.

questions.question: When flying from a high-pressure area to a low-pressure area without adjusting the altimeter, what will the altimeter indicate?
questions.correct_answer: An altitude higher than the actual altitude (the aircraft is lower than indicated)
distractors: An altitude lower than actual altitude
questions.explanation: High to low, look out below. Flying from high to low pressure without correcting the altimeter causes it to indicate higher than actual altitude, putting the aircraft dangerously lower than the pilot thinks.

questions.question: What does the green arc on the airspeed indicator represent?
distractors: Caution range — fly only in smooth air
questions.explanation: Green arc: normal operating range from VS1 (stall speed, clean) to VNO (max structural cruising speed). White arc is flap range, yellow arc is caution range.

questions.question: What speed does the bottom of the white arc on the airspeed indicator represent?
questions.correct_answer: VS0 — stall speed in landing configuration (full flaps)
distractors: VS1 — stall speed in clean configuration
questions.explanation: The white arc ranges from VS0 (stall speed in landing configuration/full flaps) at the bottom to VFE (max flap extended speed) at the top.

questions.question: Which instruments typically use a gyroscope driven by the vacuum system?
questions.explanation: The vacuum system typically drives the attitude indicator and heading indicator. The turn coordinator is usually electrically driven, providing redundancy.

questions.question: When accelerating on an east or west heading in the Northern Hemisphere, what error will the magnetic compass temporarily show?
questions.correct_answer: The compass will show a turn toward the north
distractors: The compass will show a turn toward the south
No error — acceleration errors only occur on north/south headings
The compass will oscillate rapidly between headings
questions.explanation: The ANDS memory aid: Accelerate-North, Decelerate-South. When accelerating on an east or west heading, the compass card tilts and temporarily indicates a turn toward north. When decelerating, it indicates a turn toward south. These errors are most pronounced on east/west headings and negligible on north/south headings.

questions.question: What does the vertical speed indicator (VSI) display?
questions.correct_answer: The rate of climb or descent in feet per minute
distractors: The aircraft's vertical altitude above sea level
The angle of climb or descent in degrees
questions.explanation: The VSI shows the rate of altitude change (climb or descent) in feet per minute by measuring the rate of pressure change in the static system through a calibrated leak.
